enrichment.short_description: Alarm Clock is a free, open-source alarm and timer applet for Linux desktops that use an AppIndicator (GNOME, Unity, and similar panels), letting users schedule repeatable alarms and countdown timers from a system tray icon.
enrichment.seo_title: Alarm Clock Applet 2026: Free Linux Alarm Tool Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Alarm Clock is a free, open-source GNOME/Linux applet for scheduling alarms and timers from your system tray. See features, setup, and how it compares.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Alarm Clock (also referenced as Alarm Clock Applet) is a free and open-source desktop utility for Linux systems that provides a fully-featured alarm clock through an AppIndicator, the small icon that sits in a system tray or top panel on GNOME, Unity, and similar desktop environments. It is not a commercial SaaS product and has no parent company: it is maintained entirely by volunteer developers on GitHub under the alarm-clock-applet organization, distributed under the GPL-2.0 open-source license, and written primarily in C with a CMake build system. The project supports two scheduling types, Clocks, which ring at a specific time of day and can repeat on chosen days of the week, and Timers, which count down from a specified duration, for example for cooking or work-interval reminders. When an alarm fires, the applet can play a bundled sound, launch a user-chosen audio file, or start a preferred music player, and both alarms and timers can be snoozed or dismissed directly from the tray icon's right-click menu, without needing to bring a separate window into focus. After a period of limited activity, the project was picked up by a new maintainer, tatokis, in January 2023, who ported the codebase from GTK 2 to GTK 3 (requiring GTK 3.22.30 or newer), refreshed dependency handling for libnotify, GStreamer, and Ayatana AppIndicator, and set a minimum supported baseline of Ubuntu 18.04 and comparable distributions. The most recent tagged release, version 0.4.1 ('Happy New Year'), shipped on January 9, 2023, and development continues on GitHub with several hundred commits and an active issue tracker. Because there is no company behind it, there is no pricing, subscription, or licensing fee: the source code is free to download, compile, and modify, and it is also packaged for easy installation via Launchpad PPAs and some Linux distribution repositories. Alarm Clock competes conceptually with built-in GNOME Clocks, KDE's alarm tools, or cron-based scripting, but its niche is being a lightweight, panel-native, AppIndicator-first alarm applet for users who specifically want a tray-accessible alarm rather than a full desktop application. It is best suited to Linux desktop users, particularly on Ubuntu-derived and GNOME-family distributions, who want simple, always-visible alarm and timer functionality without installing a heavier scheduling suite.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Alarm Clock
paragraphs: Alarm Clock is a small, open-source Linux desktop application that adds alarm and timer functionality to the system tray via an AppIndicator, the icon area used by GNOME, Unity, and similar panel-based desktop environments.
It is a community project, not a commercial company: the code lives in the alarm-clock-applet organization on GitHub, is licensed under GPL-2.0, and is written mostly in C using GTK 3 and CMake.
The applet is aimed at Linux users who want a lightweight, always-accessible way to set wake-up alarms, recurring reminders, or countdown timers without opening a full calendar or productivity app.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Alarm Clock distinguishes between two alarm types: Clocks, which trigger at a fixed time and can repeat on selected weekdays, and Timers, which count down from a set duration for one-off reminders.
Notifications can play a built-in sound, a custom audio file, or launch the user's preferred media player, and both alarms and timers support snoozing directly from the panel icon's right-click menu.
Because it runs as a lightweight AppIndicator process, it stays out of the way until an alarm fires, and requires no account, login, or internet connection to function.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Alarm Clock is completely free and open source under the GPL-2.0 license. There are no paid tiers, subscriptions, or in-app purchases of any kind.

Users can install it from source, from a distribution's package repository where available, or via a Launchpad PPA, and are free to inspect, modify, or redistribute the code under the terms of the GPL.

features.description: Set alarms for a specific time of day that can repeat on chosen days of the week, similar to a phone's alarm app.

features.title: Countdown timers
features.description: Create one-off timers that count down from a specified duration and notify you when time is up.

features.title: System tray integration
features.description: Runs as an AppIndicator icon in the system tray or top panel, with quick access to snooze and stop controls.

features.title: Custom alarm sounds
features.description: Choose a bundled sound, a custom audio file, or launch a preferred music player when an alarm triggers.

features.title: Snooze support
features.description: Snooze active alarms or timers directly from the panel menu without opening a separate window.

features.title: GTK 3 interface
features.description: Ported to GTK 3 in 2023, keeping the applet visually consistent with modern GNOME-based desktops.

features.title: Open-source codebase
features.description: Source available on GitHub under GPL-2.0, allowing anyone to inspect, modify, or contribute to the project.

features.title: Lightweight footprint
features.description: Written in C with minimal dependencies (GLib, GIO, libnotify, GStreamer, Ayatana AppIndicator), keeping resource usage low.

pros: Completely free and open source, with no ads, accounts, or subscriptions
Lightweight and runs quietly in the system tray until needed
Supports both repeating alarms and countdown timers in one applet
Actively maintained again as of 2023 with GTK 3 support
Simple, distraction-free interface focused purely on alarms
cons: Requires an AppIndicator-compatible desktop environment, so it does not work out of the box everywhere
No mobile app, cloud sync, or cross-device alarm support
Development pace is driven by volunteer maintainers, so updates can be infrequent
Limited documentation compared to commercial alarm or productivity apps
No built-in support features like live chat or ticketed help, only GitHub issues

faqs.question: Is Alarm Clock free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Alarm Clock is completely free and open source under the GPL-2.0 license, with no paid tiers or subscriptions.

faqs.question: What desktop environments does Alarm Clock support?
faqs.answer: It is built for Linux desktops that support an AppIndicator, such as GNOME (with the AppIndicator extension), Unity, and similar panel-based environments.

faqs.question: Who maintains Alarm Clock?
faqs.answer: It is a community-maintained open-source project hosted on GitHub under the alarm-clock-applet organization; maintainer tatokis took over active development in January 2023.

faqs.question: Does Alarm Clock require an internet connection or account?
faqs.answer: No. It runs entirely locally on your Linux desktop and does not require sign-up, login, or an internet connection.

faqs.question: Can I set recurring alarms?
faqs.answer: Yes, Alarm Clock supports Clock-type alarms that can repeat on specific days of the week, in addition to one-off countdown Timers.

faqs.question: What are the system requirements?
faqs.answer: It requires GTK 3.22.30 or newer, along with GLib, GIO, libnotify, GStreamer, and Ayatana AppIndicator libraries; the minimum supported Ubuntu version is 18.04.

faqs.question: Where can I get support or report bugs?
faqs.answer: Support and bug reports are handled through the project's GitHub issue tracker at github.com/alarm-clock-applet/alarm-clock.

faqs.question: Is Alarm Clock related to any commercial product?
faqs.answer: No. It has no parent company and is not affiliated with any commercial alarm clock brand or SaaS product.

use_cases.title: Wake-up and daily reminders
use_cases.description: Set repeatable morning alarms or recurring daily reminders that stay visible in the system tray.

use_cases.title: Work interval timers
use_cases.description: Use countdown timers for tasks like Pomodoro-style work sessions, cooking, or short breaks.

use_cases.title: Lightweight alternative to full calendar apps
use_cases.description: Provide simple alarm functionality without installing a heavier calendar or productivity suite.
use_cases.audience: Users of minimal or resource-constrained Linux setups

enrichment.short_description: Albert is a free, open-source, plugin-based keyboard launcher for Linux and macOS that lets users quickly find and open applications, files, and system actions, similar to Alfred on Mac or Spotlight.
enrichment.seo_title: Albert Launcher Review 2026: Free Linux App Launcher
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Albert is a free, open-source keyboard launcher for Linux and macOS with a plugin system. See features, plugins, and how it compares to Alfred and Spotlight.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Albert is a free, open-source, desktop-agnostic keyboard launcher written in C++ and Qt, created and primarily maintained by developer Manuel Schneider (GitHub handle ManuelSchneid3r) and released under the GNU General Public License v3.0. It is functionally comparable to Alfred on macOS or Spotlight search, giving users a single keyboard-triggered input box for launching applications, opening files and directories, performing calculations, searching the web, running system commands, and more. Albert is not a company or commercial SaaS product; it is a community open-source project hosted on GitHub under the albertlauncher organization, with an official documentation and marketing site at albertlauncher.github.io, and a Patreon page where users can optionally support ongoing development. The application's core design goal is speed, aiming to keep pace with rapid typing and return results with minimal latency, which is why it is frequently recommended as a lightweight alternative to heavier application menus or desktop search tools on Linux. Its defining architectural feature is a plugin system: Albert ships with a curated set of peer-reviewed, actively maintained plugins covering things like application launching, file search, calculator functions, and web search shortcuts, and developers can extend it further with custom plugins using Albert's C++ API, which is designed to be simple to learn for common launcher tasks. This plugin architecture means Albert can function as anything from a minimal app launcher to a full system command palette, depending on which plugins a user enables. It primarily targets Linux users across various desktop environments (it is desktop-agnostic rather than tied to GNOME or KDE specifically), and it is also available on macOS, appealing to power users who want a fast, keyboard-first way to interact with their system without relying on the mouse. Since Albert is entirely free and open source, there are no pricing tiers, subscriptions, or paid feature gates; anyone can download, compile, and modify it under the terms of the GPLv3. Compared to Alfred (macOS, freemium with a paid Powerpack) or GNOME/KDE's built-in search, Albert's appeal is that it is fully open source, cross-platform between Linux and macOS, and highly customizable through its plugin ecosystem, making it a favorite among Linux power users and developers who want an Alfred-like experience outside of Apple's ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Albert
paragraphs: Albert is a free, open-source keyboard launcher for Linux (and macOS) built with C++ and Qt, designed to give users instant, keyboard-driven access to applications, files, calculations, and system actions.
It was created by developer Manuel Schneider and is released under the GPLv3 license, with development and issue tracking hosted publicly on GitHub under the albertlauncher organization.

Albert is desktop-agnostic, meaning it is not tied to a single desktop environment like GNOME or KDE, which makes it a popular choice among Linux users who want an Alfred- or Spotlight-style launcher regardless of their setup.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, Albert provides a single input box, triggered by a global hotkey, where users can type to search and launch applications, open files and folders, run calculations, or trigger web searches.

Its plugin system is the main extensibility mechanism: Albert ships with a set of officially reviewed and maintained plugins, and developers can build additional plugins using its C++ API to add custom functionality or integrate with other tools.

Performance is a stated design priority, with the project emphasizing fast, low-latency results even under rapid typing, which is central to its appeal as a productivity tool for keyboard-centric users.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Albert is entirely free and open source under the GPLv3 license, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or locked features.
The project accepts optional financial support through a Patreon page for users who want to help fund ongoing development, but this is voluntary and not required to use any part of the application.

features.title: Global keyboard launcher
features.description: Trigger a search box with a global hotkey to instantly launch apps, files, or run actions without touching the mouse.

features.title: Plugin-based architecture
features.description: Extend functionality with a curated set of official plugins or build custom plugins using Albert's C++ API.

features.title: Application launching
features.description: Quickly search and open installed applications by name, similar to Spotlight or Alfred.

features.title: File and directory search
features.description: Find and open files and folders directly from the launcher input box.

features.title: Built-in calculator
features.description: Perform quick calculations by typing expressions directly into the launcher.

features.title: Web search shortcuts
features.description: Trigger web searches on configured search engines directly from the launcher.

features.title: Desktop-agnostic design
features.description: Works across different Linux desktop environments rather than being tied to GNOME or KDE specifically.

features.title: Cross-platform support
features.description: Available for Linux and macOS, and can be built from source on other platforms.

pros: Completely free and open source under GPLv3
Fast, keyboard-first workflow that minimizes mouse usage
Flexible plugin system that can turn it into a full command palette
Works across multiple Linux desktop environments, not tied to one
Actively maintained with a public GitHub repository and documentation site
cons: Requires manual setup of plugins to unlock its full potential, unlike more polished commercial launchers
No official Windows support
Community-driven support model (GitHub issues) rather than dedicated customer service
Learning curve for users unfamiliar with keyboard-driven launcher workflows
Plugin quality and documentation can vary for community-contributed extensions

faqs.question: Is Albert free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Albert is completely free and open source under the GPLv3 license, with no paid plans.

faqs.question: What platforms does Albert support?
faqs.answer: Albert supports Linux across multiple desktop environments and also has a macOS build; there is no official Windows version.

faqs.question: Who created Albert?
faqs.answer: Albert was created by developer Manuel Schneider and is maintained as an open-source project on GitHub.

faqs.question: How is Albert different from Alfred or Spotlight?
faqs.answer: Albert offers similar keyboard-launcher functionality but is free, open source, and available on Linux, whereas Alfred is macOS-only with a paid Powerpack and Spotlight is built into macOS.

faqs.question: Can I extend Albert with custom functionality?
faqs.answer: Yes, Albert has a plugin system and a C++ API that lets developers build custom plugins beyond the officially maintained set.

faqs.question: Does Albert require an account or internet connection?
faqs.answer: No, Albert runs locally on your machine and does not require an account or internet connection for its core launcher functionality.

faqs.question: What license is Albert released under?
faqs.answer: Albert is released under the GNU General Public License v3.0 (GPLv3).

faqs.question: Where can I get help or report issues with Albert?
faqs.answer: Support happens through the project's GitHub repository issue tracker and community documentation at albertlauncher.github.io.

use_cases.description: Maintain a consistent launcher experience across different Linux desktop environments or distributions.
use_cases.audience: Users who switch between multiple Linux setups

enrichment.short_description: AlienVault was a cybersecurity and threat intelligence company known for its Unified Security Management (USM) platform and the open-source OSSIM SIEM; it was acquired by AT&T in 2018, rebranded as AT&T Cybersecurity, and its products are now operated by LevelBlue, a standalone managed cybersecurity company launched in 2024.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AlienVault is now part of LevelBlue, the AT&T-linked cybersecurity firm. Learn the history, USM and OSSIM products, and current branding for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AlienVault is the original name of a threat intelligence and security management company whose products have since changed corporate ownership multiple times, which is important for anyone researching the brand today. The underlying technology traces back to 2003, when developers Dominique Karg, Julio Casal, and later Alberto Roman and Ignacio Cabrera began OSSIM (Open Source Security Information Management), a free, open-source SIEM (security information and event management) project. In 2007, this team founded AlienVault as a commercial company headquartered in San Mateo, California, with additional offices in Madrid and Granada, Spain, and Cork, Ireland, building a paid product called Unified Security Management (USM) on top of the OSSIM foundation. USM combined asset discovery, vulnerability assessment, intrusion detection, behavioral monitoring, and SIEM correlation into a single platform, and AlienVault also operated the Open Threat Exchange (OTX), a crowdsourced threat intelligence sharing community. AlienVault raised roughly 116 million dollars in venture funding over its life as an independent company before AT&T Communications acquired it in a deal announced in July 2018 and completed in August 2018; AlienVault was folded into AT&T and rebranded as AT&T Cybersecurity, continuing to sell USM (later USM Anywhere, a cloud-native SIEM and XDR platform) alongside AT&T's broader managed security services. The most recent major change occurred in May 2024, when AT&T spun off its cybersecurity services business into LevelBlue, a new standalone company formed as a joint venture between AT&T and private equity firm WillJam Ventures, with WillJam taking majority ownership and AT&T retaining a minority stake and board seat; LevelBlue is headquartered in Dallas, Texas, employs over 1,000 people across roughly 10 countries, and is led by WillJam Ventures founder Robert McCullen. As part of this transition, LevelBlue announced the retirement of the free, open-source OSSIM product, ending vendor updates, maintenance, and security patches as of December 31, 2024, in order to concentrate resources on its commercial USM Anywhere open XDR platform and broader managed detection and response (MDR), consulting, and threat intelligence services. So while the alienvault.com domain still exists and redirects to AT&T Cybersecurity and LevelBlue properties, the AlienVault name today is best understood as a legacy brand: its technology and customer base now live under LevelBlue, and prospective buyers evaluating 'AlienVault' products in 2026 are effectively evaluating LevelBlue's USM Anywhere and managed cybersecurity portfolio, sold on a custom, quote-based enterprise pricing model typical of managed security service providers.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AlienVault
paragraphs: AlienVault was a cybersecurity company founded in 2007, built around the open-source OSSIM SIEM project, that developed the commercial Unified Security Management (USM) platform combining SIEM, intrusion detection, vulnerability scanning, and asset discovery in one product.

AT&T Communications acquired AlienVault in 2018, rebranding it as AT&T Cybersecurity, and in May 2024 AT&T spun the business off into LevelBlue, a standalone joint venture with WillJam Ventures, headquartered in Dallas, Texas.

As of 2026, the AlienVault name is a legacy brand: its products, customer relationships, and threat intelligence operations now run under LevelBlue, and the free OSSIM tool was formally retired at the end of 2024.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The former AlienVault, now LevelBlue's core commercial platform, USM Anywhere, is a cloud-native SIEM and open XDR system that unifies log collection, threat detection, vulnerability assessment, and incident response in one console.

It draws on the Open Threat Exchange (OTX), a crowdsourced threat intelligence community originally built by AlienVault, to enrich detections with up-to-date indicators of compromise contributed by a global security community.

LevelBlue supplements the technology platform with managed detection and response (MDR), 24/7 security operations center (SOC) support, and cybersecurity consulting services, positioning it as a full-service managed security provider rather than a standalone software vendor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Pricing for USM Anywhere and LevelBlue's managed cybersecurity services is not published publicly and is quoted on a custom, per-organization basis, typical for enterprise security and managed detection and response contracts.

The free, open-source OSSIM product that AlienVault originally offered has been officially retired since December 31, 2024, and no longer receives updates or support, so it is no longer a free entry point into the ecosystem.

features.title: Unified Security Management (USM Anywhere)
features.description: Cloud-native SIEM and open XDR platform combining log management, correlation, and detection in one console.

features.title: Threat intelligence via OTX
features.description: Detections are enriched using the Open Threat Exchange, a crowdsourced global threat intelligence feed originally built by AlienVault.

features.title: Asset discovery and vulnerability assessment
features.description: Identifies devices on the network and scans for known vulnerabilities as part of the unified platform.

features.title: Intrusion detection
features.description: Monitors network and host activity for signs of malicious behavior and known attack patterns.

features.title: Managed detection and response (MDR)
features.description: LevelBlue offers managed services where its analysts monitor, triage, and respond to threats on a customer's behalf.

features.title: 24/7 security operations center support
features.description: Access to round-the-clock SOC monitoring and incident response services.

features.title: Cybersecurity consulting
features.description: Advisory services covering security strategy, compliance, and incident readiness.

features.title: Legacy OSSIM heritage
features.description: The commercial platform is built on the foundation of OSSIM, the open-source SIEM project AlienVault originally commercialized, though OSSIM itself is now retired.

pros: Long history and deep threat intelligence data via the Open Threat Exchange community
Combines SIEM, XDR, vulnerability management, and MDR under one vendor
Backed by a large, well-resourced organization (LevelBlue, formerly AT&T Cybersecurity)
Established enterprise and mid-market customer base with proven managed security track record
Options for both self-managed software and fully managed security services
cons: Confusing brand history across AlienVault, AT&T Cybersecurity, and LevelBlue can make current positioning hard to research
The free, open-source OSSIM product has been retired, removing a former no-cost entry point
Pricing is not public and requires a custom sales quote, which can slow evaluation
As with most enterprise SIEM/XDR platforms, deployment and tuning can require significant security expertise
Users researching 'AlienVault' online may land on outdated information given the multiple rebrands

faqs.question: Is AlienVault still a company?
faqs.answer: Not as an independent company. AlienVault was acquired by AT&T in 2018, rebranded AT&T Cybersecurity, and in May 2024 its business became part of LevelBlue, a standalone joint venture between AT&T and WillJam Ventures.

faqs.question: What happened to OSSIM?
faqs.answer: OSSIM, the free open-source SIEM AlienVault was built on, was officially retired by LevelBlue at the end of December 2024, ending updates, maintenance, and security patches.

faqs.question: What is USM Anywhere?
faqs.answer: USM Anywhere is the cloud-native SIEM and open XDR platform that evolved from AlienVault's original Unified Security Management product, now sold under LevelBlue.

faqs.question: Who owns LevelBlue?
faqs.answer: LevelBlue is a joint venture between AT&T, which holds a minority stake and board seat, and private equity firm WillJam Ventures, which holds the majority stake.

faqs.question: Where is LevelBlue headquartered?
faqs.answer: LevelBlue is headquartered in Dallas, Texas, and employs over 1,000 people across roughly 10 countries.

faqs.question: Is AlienVault's software still free?
faqs.answer: No. The free OSSIM product is retired; the current commercial platform, USM Anywhere, and LevelBlue's managed services are sold under custom enterprise pricing.

faqs.question: What is the Open Threat Exchange (OTX)?
faqs.answer: OTX is a crowdsourced threat intelligence sharing platform originally built by AlienVault, still used to enrich detections in LevelBlue's current products.

faqs.question: When was AlienVault founded?
faqs.answer: AlienVault was founded in 2007, building on the open-source OSSIM project that began in 2003.

use_cases.title: Enterprise threat detection and response

use_cases.title: Managed security for lean IT teams

use_cases.audience: Mid-market companies without a dedicated SOC

use_cases.title: Compliance-driven security monitoring

use_cases.audience: Regulated industries such as finance and healthcare

enrichment.short_description: Alloy (alloy.ai) is a retail and supply chain intelligence platform that unifies point-of-sale, inventory, and distributor data for consumer brands and retailers, powering demand forecasting, out-of-stock prevention, and retail execution.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Alloy.ai unifies POS and inventory data for consumer brands. See features, demand forecasting tools, pricing model, and who it is built for in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Alloy, operating at the domain alloy.ai, is a retail and consumer goods analytics company founded in 2016 and headquartered in San Francisco, California. It should not be confused with the identity-verification fintech company also named Alloy at alloy.com; alloy.ai is an entirely separate business focused on retail execution, demand planning, and inventory intelligence for consumer brands, distributors, and retailers. The platform is a cloud-native data layer that ingests point-of-sale (POS), enterprise resource planning (ERP), distributor, and supplier data through more than 850 pre-built connectors, then normalizes that data by mapping SKUs, units, and timeframes so brands can get a single, reconciled view of sell-through and inventory across thousands of retail locations. On top of this unified data foundation, Alloy.ai offers machine-learning-powered demand forecasting at the SKU and store level, claiming accuracy improvements of up to 35 percent when incorporating real-time POS signals, with forecast outputs exportable to enterprise planning systems such as SAP IBP, Blue Yonder, and o9. The platform also provides real-time inventory visibility across more than 20,000 retail store locations, and has more recently added agentic AI capabilities that continuously monitor SKU-level risk (such as stockouts or phantom inventory) and prepare execution-ready recommended actions, like replenishment or reorder emails, for retail execution and sales teams to review and approve rather than requiring manual analysis. Alloy.ai is a private, venture-backed company that has raised a Series A funding round (specific amount not publicly disclosed in available sources) and has previously been recognized as an Inc. 5000 honoree. Employee counts across data providers put the company in the range of roughly 60 to 70 people, indicating a focused, mid-sized team rather than a large enterprise vendor. Its customers are consumer packaged goods (CPG) brands and retailers who need to reconcile fragmented sell-through data from multiple retail partners and distributors into one system of record. As with most B2B enterprise data platforms in this category, Alloy.ai does not publish self-serve pricing; prospective customers must request a custom quote, and pricing is understood to scale with the number of retail connections, SKUs, and data volume involved. Competitors and adjacent tools in this space include other retail analytics and CPG data platforms, as well as broader supply chain planning suites like Blue Yonder and o9, with Alloy.ai's differentiation resting on its breadth of pre-built retail data connectors and its focus on turning fragmented POS data into actionable forecasting and execution workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Alloy
paragraphs: Alloy, at the domain alloy.ai, is a retail and supply chain intelligence platform founded in 2016 and based in San Francisco, built specifically for consumer brands and retailers that need to make sense of fragmented point-of-sale and inventory data.

It is a distinct company from the identity-verification platform also named Alloy at alloy.com; alloy.ai focuses exclusively on retail analytics, demand forecasting, and inventory management, with no relation to fintech identity verification.

The platform connects to point-of-sale, ERP, distributor, and supplier systems through hundreds of pre-built connectors, normalizing the resulting data into a unified view of sell-through and stock across the retail supply chain.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Alloy.ai's core capability is data unification: it ingests and reconciles POS, ERP, and distributor data across thousands of retail locations, mapping SKUs and timeframes so brands are not stuck manually merging spreadsheets from different retail partners.

On top of that data layer sits machine-learning demand forecasting at the SKU and store level, which the company positions as materially more accurate than traditional forecasting when incorporating live point-of-sale signals, with results exportable to major supply chain planning systems.

More recently, Alloy.ai has introduced agentic AI features that continuously watch inventory risk across SKUs and locations and generate execution-ready recommendations, such as replenishment actions, for retail execution teams to review.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Alloy.ai does not publish self-serve pricing on its website; as an enterprise B2B data and analytics platform, pricing is provided through a custom quote based on factors such as the number of retail connections, SKUs, and data volume a brand requires.
Prospective customers typically need to request a demo or consultation with the Alloy.ai sales team to receive a proposal tailored to their retail footprint.

features.title: 850 plus retail data connectors
features.description: Pre-built integrations with POS, ERP, distributor, and supplier systems to ingest retail sell-through data at scale.

features.title: SKU and store-level demand forecasting

features.title: Agentic AI monitoring
features.description: AI agents continuously monitor SKUs for risk and prepare execution-ready recommended actions for teams to approve.

features.title: Planning system integration
features.description: Forecast outputs can be exported to enterprise planning platforms such as SAP IBP, Blue Yonder, and o9.

features.title: Retail execution workflows

features.title: Data normalization and SKU mapping
features.description: Reconciles inconsistent SKU, unit, and timeframe formats across different retail partners into one clean dataset.

features.title: CPG-focused analytics
features.description: Purpose-built reporting and analytics tailored to consumer packaged goods brands rather than generic BI dashboards.

pros: Deep library of pre-built retail data connectors reduces manual data integration work
Demand forecasting incorporates real-time POS data rather than relying only on historical shipment data
Agentic AI features move beyond dashboards toward recommended, execution-ready actions
Integrates with major enterprise supply chain planning systems
Focused specifically on CPG and retail use cases rather than being a generic analytics tool
cons: Pricing is not public, requiring a sales conversation before cost can be evaluated
Primarily built for brands with meaningful retail footprint, likely less suited to very small businesses
As a data-integration-heavy platform, initial setup and connector configuration can take time
Company size is relatively modest for an enterprise data platform, which may factor into evaluating long-term support capacity
Public information on funding and financials is limited, making external validation harder

faqs.question: Is Alloy.ai the same company as Alloy the identity verification platform?
faqs.answer: No. Alloy.ai is a separate company focused on retail and supply chain analytics; the identity-verification fintech company also named Alloy operates at the different domain alloy.com.

faqs.question: When was Alloy.ai founded?
faqs.answer: Alloy.ai was founded in 2016 and is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: What does Alloy.ai do?
faqs.answer: It is a retail and supply chain intelligence platform that unifies point-of-sale, ERP, and distributor data for consumer brands, powering demand forecasting, inventory visibility, and retail execution.

faqs.question: Does Alloy.ai offer public pricing?
faqs.answer: No, pricing is custom and quote-based, typical for enterprise B2B data and analytics platforms.

faqs.question: What planning systems does Alloy.ai integrate with?
faqs.answer: It can export forecasting outputs to systems such as SAP IBP, Blue Yonder, and o9.

faqs.question: Who uses Alloy.ai?
faqs.answer: Primarily consumer packaged goods (CPG) brands and retailers that need to consolidate fragmented retail sell-through and inventory data.

faqs.question: Has Alloy.ai raised funding?
faqs.answer: Yes, Alloy.ai has raised a Series A funding round, though the specific amount is not widely disclosed in public sources.

faqs.question: How many retail locations does Alloy.ai cover?
faqs.answer: The platform provides real-time inventory visibility across more than 20,000 retail store locations.

use_cases.title: Unifying multi-retailer sell-through data
use_cases.description: Consolidate POS and inventory data from many retail partners into a single reconciled dataset.
use_cases.audience: CPG brands selling through multiple retailers

use_cases.title: SKU-level demand forecasting
use_cases.description: Improve forecast accuracy at the SKU and store level using real-time point-of-sale signals.
use_cases.audience: Demand planning and supply chain teams

use_cases.title: Out-of-stock and phantom inventory prevention
use_cases.description: Monitor inventory risk across thousands of stores and act on AI-generated recommendations before stockouts occur.
use_cases.audience: Retail execution and field sales teams

enrichment.short_description: AllRatesToday is a developer-focused REST API providing real-time and historical currency exchange rates for over 160 currencies, plus a free web-based currency converter and rate comparison tools.
enrichment.seo_title: AllRatesToday Review 2026: Free Currency Exchange API
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AllRatesToday offers a free and paid REST API for real-time and historical exchange rates across 160 plus currencies. See plans, features, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AllRatesToday is a currency exchange rate data service built primarily as a developer API, offering real-time and historical foreign exchange rates for more than 160 currencies through a REST API, official SDKs (JavaScript/TypeScript, Python, PHP, and a React component library), and a consumer-facing currency converter tool on its website. Its rates are sourced from institutional data providers including Reuters (Refinitiv) and interbank market feeds, with the company stating that exchange rates are refreshed roughly every 60 seconds and that it targets 99.99 percent uptime, backed by Cloudflare infrastructure. The product is aimed at developers and businesses embedding currency conversion into e-commerce checkout flows, finance dashboards, travel applications, accounting software, and any system that needs to display or calculate amounts across multiple currencies, and it also publishes a public currency-converter page and rate-comparison content (for example comparing money transfer providers for pairs like EUR to USD) aimed at everyday consumers. AllRatesToday follows a freemium pricing structure: a Free plan offers one API key with a rate limit of 10 requests per minute, real-time and latest rates only, and is intended for non-commercial use with no credit card required. Paid tiers scale from there: a Small plan at roughly 4.99 euros per month (or 49.99 euros per year) includes 5,000 requests per month, one API key, historical rate access, and permission for commercial use; a Medium plan, the provider's recommended tier, costs roughly 9.99 euros per month (99.99 euros annually) and raises the limit to 10,000 requests per month with unlimited rate access and the full Convert endpoint; and a Large plan at roughly 49.99 euros per month (499 euros annually) supports 100,000 requests per month, three API keys, and priority email support, aimed at higher-traffic production applications. Annual billing across paid tiers offers roughly a 17 percent discount, equivalent to about two free months. Concrete public details about the company behind AllRatesToday, such as a specific founding year, headquarters location, or team size, were not found in available sources; the site's footer displays a broad copyright range, but this alone is not a reliable indicator of the company's actual founding date, so that detail is treated as unverified rather than stated as fact. As a currency-data API, AllRatesToday competes with services like Open Exchange Rates, exchangerate-api.com, Fixer, and similar forex data providers, differentiating primarily on its combination of a genuinely free entry tier, multiple official SDKs, and relatively low-cost paid plans for smaller applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AllRatesToday
paragraphs: AllRatesToday is a currency exchange rate service centered on a developer REST API that provides real-time and historical foreign exchange data for more than 160 currencies, alongside official SDKs for JavaScript/TypeScript, Python, and PHP, plus a React component library.
In addition to the API, the site offers consumer-facing tools such as a free currency converter and pages comparing money-transfer providers for specific currency pairs.
Its exchange rate data is sourced from institutional feeds, including Reuters (Refinitiv) and interbank markets, with rates refreshed on a roughly 60-second cycle.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core product is the exchange rate API itself, supporting latest rates, historical rate lookups, and currency conversion endpoints across 160 plus currencies.
Developers can integrate quickly using official SDKs in multiple languages, and the site also provides interactive API documentation and a live API playground for testing calls before writing code.
For non-developer audiences, AllRatesToday also runs a public currency converter and rate-comparison pages intended for everyday users checking exchange rates or comparing transfer providers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: AllRatesToday offers a genuine Free plan with 10 requests per minute and access to real-time and latest rates, intended for non-commercial use and requiring no credit card.

Paid plans scale by monthly request volume: Small at about 4.99 euros per month (5,000 requests, historical data, commercial use allowed), Medium at about 9.99 euros per month (10,000 requests, full API access including the Convert endpoint), and Large at about 49.99 euros per month (100,000 requests, three API keys, priority support), with roughly a 17 percent discount for annual billing.

features.description: Larger plans support multiple API keys, useful for separating staging and production environments.

features.description: Rates sourced from Reuters (Refinitiv) and interbank feeds rather than unverified aggregator data.

pros: Genuine free tier with no credit card required, unusual among forex data APIs
Low-cost entry-level paid plans starting under 5 euros per month
Multiple official SDKs reduce integration time for common languages and frameworks
Institutional-grade data sourcing from Reuters and interbank feeds
Both a developer API and consumer-facing tools available on the same platform
cons: Free tier is limited to real-time and latest rates only, excluding historical data
Rate limits on lower tiers (10 requests per minute on Free) may be restrictive for higher-traffic use
Public information about the company behind the service, such as founding date or team size, is limited
As a smaller, less established provider compared to major forex data vendors, long-term reliability track record is harder to verify externally
Commercial use requires a paid plan even at low request volumes

faqs.question: Is AllRatesToday free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, it offers a Free plan with 10 requests per minute for real-time and latest rates, though commercial use and historical data require a paid plan.

faqs.question: How many currencies does AllRatesToday support?
faqs.answer: It supports more than 160 currencies for both live and historical exchange rate data.

faqs.question: Where does AllRatesToday get its exchange rate data?
faqs.answer: Rates are sourced from institutional providers including Reuters (Refinitiv) and interbank market feeds, refreshed roughly every 60 seconds.

faqs.question: What SDKs does AllRatesToday offer?
faqs.answer: Official SDKs are available for JavaScript/TypeScript, Python, and PHP, along with a React component library.

faqs.question: What is the cheapest paid plan?
faqs.answer: The Small plan costs about 4.99 euros per month (or 49.99 euros per year) and includes 5,000 requests per month and historical rate access.

faqs.question: Can I use AllRatesToday for commercial applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, but commercial use requires at least the Small paid plan; the Free plan is intended for non-commercial use only.

faqs.question: Does AllRatesToday offer a currency converter for non-developers?
faqs.answer: Yes, the site includes a free, public currency converter tool alongside its developer API.

faqs.question: What is included in the Large plan?
faqs.answer: The Large plan, at about 49.99 euros per month, includes 100,000 requests per month, three API keys, and priority email support.

enrichment.short_description: AlphaAI (alphai.io) is a financial news API built for AI agents and trading bots, delivering relevance-scored, ticker-linked news with sentiment analysis via REST API and MCP server for AI tools like Claude and Cursor.
enrichment.seo_title: AlphaAI Review 2026: Financial News API for AI Agents
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AlphaAI is a financial news API built for AI agents and trading bots, with relevance scores and sentiment. See pricing, SDKs, and MCP server details.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AlphaAI, accessed via alphai.io, is a developer-focused financial news API purpose-built for AI agents, trading bots, and automated dashboards rather than for human readers browsing a news site. Its core offering is a REST API (documented at alphai.io/developers) that delivers relevance-scored, ticker-linked financial news: each article is tagged with a relevance score from 1 to 10, a topic category, and per-ticker sentiment analysis that includes written reasoning rather than just a numeric label, which is intended to make the feed directly consumable by large language models and automated trading logic. The service emphasizes freshness and coverage of market-moving events, stating that new articles land in the feed within about an hour of publication and that SEC filings are converted into feed entries in a median of around six minutes. Beyond the plain REST API, AlphaAI also offers a hosted Model Context Protocol (MCP) server at mcp.alphai.io, which is MIT licensed and uses OAuth 2.1 with Dynamic Client Registration and PKCE for authentication, allowing the news feed to be plugged directly into AI agent tools such as Claude Desktop, Cursor, and other MCP-compatible clients without requiring self-hosting. Official client SDKs are provided for Python (with both synchronous and asynchronous support) and TypeScript, the latter shipping with zero runtime dependencies, making integration straightforward for developers building trading bots, research tools, or AI-agent-facing financial applications. AlphaAI uses a freemium, usage-tiered pricing model: a Free plan provides one API key with a burst limit of 20 requests per minute and a cap of 100 requests per day, sufficient for evaluation and prototyping with no credit card required and no access to alerting features; a Basic plan at 2.99 US dollars per month raises limits to 60 requests per minute and 10,000 requests per day and adds alert functionality; and a Pro plan at 9.99 US dollars per month further raises limits to 300 requests per minute and 100,000 requests per day, adds alerts, and supports larger page sizes (up to 50 results per call, versus 10 on the lower tiers) for bulk queries. Public information about the company behind AlphaAI, including a specific founding year, headquarters, or team size, was not available in the sources reviewed, so those fields are left unverified rather than guessed. AlphaAI's positioning sits alongside other financial data and news APIs such as Alpha Vantage and StockNewsAPI, but differentiates itself specifically around AI-agent and MCP-native consumption, relevance scoring, and per-ticker sentiment reasoning rather than being a general-purpose market data API.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AlphaAI
paragraphs: AlphaAI, at alphai.io, is a financial news API designed specifically for consumption by AI agents and trading bots rather than by human readers, providing relevance-scored, ticker-linked news with sentiment analysis.

Its developer platform, documented at alphai.io/developers, offers a REST API along with Python and TypeScript SDKs, and a hosted MCP server that plugs the same news feed directly into AI agent tools like Claude Desktop and Cursor.

The service is built around speed and structure: articles are enriched with relevance scores, topic tags, and per-ticker sentiment reasoning shortly after publication, aiming to make raw financial news immediately usable by automated systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Every article in the feed carries a 1 to 10 relevance score, a topic category, and ticker-level sentiment with written reasoning, rather than a simple positive, negative, or neutral label, which helps downstream AI systems weigh the news appropriately.
The feed is built for low latency, with new articles typically appearing within about an hour of publication and SEC filings converted into feed rows in a median of roughly six minutes.

The MIT-licensed, hosted MCP server at mcp.alphai.io lets the news feed be consumed directly by AI agent applications using standard MCP tooling and OAuth 2.1 authentication, without needing to self-host any infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: AlphaAI offers a free tier with one API key, 20 requests per minute, and 100 requests per day, requiring no credit card and suitable for evaluation and prototyping.

Paid tiers include Basic at 2.99 US dollars per month (60 requests per minute, 10,000 requests per day, alerts enabled) and Pro at 9.99 US dollars per month (300 requests per minute, 100,000 requests per day, alerts, and larger bulk page sizes).

features.description: New articles typically appear in the feed within about an hour of publication.

features.title: Rapid SEC filing coverage
features.description: SEC filings are converted into feed entries in a median of about six minutes after filing.

features.title: Hosted MCP server
features.description: A Model Context Protocol server at mcp.alphai.io lets AI agent tools like Claude Desktop and Cursor consume the feed directly.

features.title: Python and TypeScript SDKs
features.description: Official client libraries with sync and async support in Python and a zero-dependency TypeScript client.

features.title: Alerting on paid plans
features.description: Basic and Pro plans add alert functionality for monitoring specific tickers or events.

features.title: Scalable request limits
features.description: Rate limits scale from 20 requests per minute on Free up to 300 requests per minute on Pro for production workloads.

pros: Purpose-built for AI agents and trading bots rather than repurposed general news content
Genuinely free tier available for evaluation with no credit card required
Low-cost paid tiers starting at under 3 US dollars per month
Native MCP server support simplifies integration with AI agent tools like Claude and Cursor
Sentiment analysis includes written reasoning, not just a numeric or categorical score
cons: Free tier's 100 requests per day cap is very limited for anything beyond prototyping
No alerting functionality on the Free plan
Public company information (founding year, team size, headquarters) is not readily available
As a narrower, specialized API, it lacks broader market data (pricing, fundamentals) found in larger financial data platforms
Relatively new-looking product with a smaller public track record compared to established financial data providers

faqs.question: What does AlphaAI do?
faqs.answer: AlphaAI provides a financial news API delivering relevance-scored, ticker-linked news with sentiment analysis, built specifically for AI agents and trading bots.

faqs.question: Is there a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Free plan includes one API key with 20 requests per minute and 100 requests per day, with no credit card required.

faqs.question: How much do paid plans cost?
faqs.answer: Basic costs 2.99 US dollars per month and Pro costs 9.99 US dollars per month, with higher rate limits and alerting on both.

faqs.question: Does AlphaAI support AI agent tools like Claude or Cursor?
faqs.answer: Yes, AlphaAI runs a hosted MCP server at mcp.alphai.io that lets MCP-compatible AI agent tools consume the news feed directly.

faqs.question: What SDKs are available?
faqs.answer: Official SDKs are provided for Python, with synchronous and asynchronous support, and TypeScript, which has zero runtime dependencies.

faqs.question: How fresh is the news data?
faqs.answer: New articles typically appear within about an hour of publication, and SEC filings are converted into feed entries in a median of roughly six minutes.

faqs.question: What is the sentiment analysis based on?
faqs.answer: Each article includes per-ticker sentiment analysis with written reasoning, in addition to a 1 to 10 relevance score and topic category.

faqs.question: Is the MCP server open source?
faqs.answer: The hosted MCP server component is MIT licensed and uses OAuth 2.1 with Dynamic Client Registration and PKCE for authentication.

enrichment.short_description: The website manubes.de belongs to manubes, a cloud-based industrial production management (Industry 4.0) platform built by German software company inray Industriesoftware GmbH, not a WordPress translation plugin; research confirmed no product literally named this exists at this domain.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: manubes.de is manubes, a German cloud platform for industrial production management by inray Industriesoftware. See features, modules, and pricing for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: This entry covers the website manubes.de, which research shows is not a WordPress plugin adding a 'this content is also available in German' notice. Instead, manubes.de is the site for manubes, a cloud-based industrial production management platform (in the Industry 4.0 or manufacturing execution category) built by inray Industriesoftware GmbH, a German industrial software company founded in 1995 and headquartered in Schenefeld, Germany, with roughly 70 employees. inray has spent decades building software for connectivity, automation, and Industry 4.0 in manufacturing, and manubes is its more recent cloud platform product: it entered a pre-access phase in November 2023 and had its full public launch in 2024, after being described in company communications as the result of years of development. Functionally, manubes lets manufacturers structure, monitor, and manage production data, systems, and processes entirely in the cloud, accessible from a web browser on desktop or mobile devices. Its core toolkit is built around three no-code design tools: a Datamodel Designer for structuring production data, a Page Designer for building custom real-time dashboards and shopfloor displays (including Overall Equipment Effectiveness, or OEE, visualizations), and a Workflow Designer for automating production processes via drag-and-drop logic, without requiring custom coding. The platform connects to both legacy and modern factory equipment and IT systems using industry-standard protocols including OPC UA, MQTT, and REST, and supports automated data capture methods like QR-code and barcode scanning, mobile push notifications for production events, digital work instructions, shift documentation, and an AI-assisted data-preparation and analysis assistant. Its named enterprise customers reportedly include large industrial brands such as TESA, Volkswagen, WAGO, Tetra Pak, and Goodyear, positioning manubes as a serious enterprise manufacturing tool rather than a small utility. No public pricing is listed on the manubes or inray websites; prospective customers are directed to request a free trial or a custom quote, consistent with typical enterprise industrial-software sales motions. Because the task brief for this listing described manubes.de as hosting a WordPress plugin by the same name, and no such plugin could be found on WordPress.org, in inray's product pages, or in general web search, this entry instead documents manubes.de accurately as researched: an Industry 4.0 production management SaaS platform, not a translation or multilingual notice plugin, and readers looking for a German-language WordPress translation tool should look elsewhere.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is manubes.de
paragraphs: Research into the URL manubes.de found that it is the website for manubes, a cloud-based industrial production management platform aimed at manufacturers, built by the German company inray Industriesoftware GmbH.

It is not a WordPress plugin or a tool related to adding 'also available in German' language notices to websites; no such plugin could be found associated with the manubes.de domain or the company behind it.

inray Industriesoftware GmbH was founded in 1995 and is headquartered in Schenefeld, Germany, with roughly 70 employees, and has spent decades building software for Industry 4.0, connectivity, and automation in manufacturing before launching manubes as its newer cloud platform product around 2024.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: manubes centers on three no-code design tools: a Datamodel Designer for structuring production data, a Page Designer for building real-time dashboards and shopfloor displays, and a Workflow Designer for automating production processes without custom code.

The platform connects to shop-floor equipment and IT systems using OPC UA, MQTT, and REST protocols, and supports data capture methods such as QR-code and barcode scanning alongside mobile push notifications for production events.

Additional capabilities include Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) visualization, automated production reporting, digital work instructions, shift documentation, and an AI-assisted data preparation and analysis feature.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: No public pricing is listed for manubes; as an enterprise industrial software platform, access is arranged through a free trial request followed by a custom quote based on a manufacturer's specific deployment needs.
The company's named customers, including large industrial brands, suggest manubes is positioned and priced as an enterprise-grade platform rather than a low-cost self-serve tool.

pros: Backed by inray, a company with roughly three decades of industrial software experience
No-code design tools lower the barrier to building custom dashboards and workflows
Broad protocol support (OPC UA, MQTT, REST) for connecting varied factory equipment
Named enterprise customers in heavy industry suggest proven production-grade reliability
Cloud-based architecture allows browser and mobile access without on-premises infrastructure
cons: No public pricing, requiring a sales conversation to understand cost
As a specialized industrial platform, it is not relevant to general consumer or small-business use cases
manubes as a distinct cloud product is relatively new (launched around 2024), with a shorter independent track record than the parent company
Primarily German-market rooted, which may affect support availability and community size outside Europe
Requires shop-floor system integration work (OPC UA, MQTT, etc.) to realize full value, which is a nontrivial technical lift

faqs.question: Is manubes.de a WordPress plugin?
faqs.answer: No. Research found no WordPress plugin associated with manubes.de; the domain hosts manubes, a cloud-based industrial production management platform.

faqs.question: Who makes manubes?
faqs.answer: manubes is developed by inray Industriesoftware GmbH, a German company founded in 1995 and headquartered in Schenefeld, Germany.

faqs.question: What does manubes do?
faqs.answer: It is a cloud platform that lets manufacturers structure, monitor, and manage production data, systems, and processes, using no-code design tools for dashboards and workflow automation.

faqs.question: When did manubes launch?
faqs.answer: manubes entered a pre-access phase in November 2023 and had its full public launch in 2024, though its parent company inray has operated since 1995.

faqs.question: Is manubes pricing public?
faqs.answer: No, pricing is not publicly listed; interested manufacturers can request a free trial and receive a custom quote.

faqs.question: What protocols does manubes support for equipment integration?
faqs.answer: It supports OPC UA, MQTT, and REST for connecting to legacy and modern factory equipment and IT systems.

faqs.question: Who are manubes' customers?
faqs.answer: Reported enterprise customers include large industrial brands such as TESA, Volkswagen, WAGO, Tetra Pak, and Goodyear.

faqs.question: Is manubes available in English?
faqs.answer: Yes, the manubes and inray websites offer both German and English language versions.

enrichment.short_description: Alwaysdata is a French web hosting company offering shared and managed cloud hosting for developers, with a permanent free tier and paid plans supporting over 15 programming languages, unlimited sites, and daily backups.
enrichment.seo_title: Alwaysdata Review 2026: French Developer Web Hosting
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Alwaysdata is a French developer-focused web host with a free tier and paid cloud plans. See pricing, supported languages, and features for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Alwaysdata is a French web hosting company, operating as Alwaysdata SARL and headquartered at 91 rue du Faubourg Saint Honore in Paris, France. The company has operated since 2006, originally growing out of engineers working on various web technology projects including content management systems and mailing tools, before consolidating in 2012 into a team fully dedicated to building and running the alwaysdata hosting platform, which it now describes as a European cloud aimed at developers, agencies, and organizations that want data hosted within France and the EU. Alwaysdata targets a developer-first audience: its platform supports more than 15 programming languages and runtimes, including PHP, Python, Ruby, Node.js, Java, Go, and Perl, and gives users shell access, multiple database engine options (such as MySQL, PostgreSQL, and MongoDB, commonly offered by developer-focused hosts), and meaningful control over their hosting environment while the underlying infrastructure remains managed by Alwaysdata. The company's pricing follows a freemium model with three tiers. The Free plan is a genuinely permanent, no-cost tier offering 1 GB of SSD storage, 256 MB of RAM, a quarter-core CPU share, three days of backups, custom-domain email, and hosting on an alwaysdata.net subdomain, aimed at hobby projects, students, and evaluation. The Plus plan, described as managed cloud hosting, starts at 5 euros per month (excluding VAT) and scales up to larger configurations offering as much as 500 GB of SSD storage, 8 GB of RAM, 8 CPU cores, and up to 30 days of backups, with unlimited sites, email addresses, and databases under custom domains, aimed at freelancers, agencies, and small to mid-sized businesses running production sites. The Max plan is a dedicated or private-server tier starting at 369 euros per month (excluding VAT), scaling up to 15 TB of SSD storage, up to 2 TB of RAM, and up to 128 CPU cores, aimed at large organizations, public services, and SaaS companies that need guaranteed, dedicated performance rather than a shared or managed-cloud environment. Alwaysdata markets itself specifically toward web agencies managing multiple client sites, public administrations and services requiring French data residency, and companies hosting the infrastructure behind their own SaaS products, positioning it against both budget shared hosts and larger cloud providers by emphasizing transparent pricing (no hidden fees for standard features like database access or additional programming languages), EU-based data centers, and a developer-friendly toolset. The company keeps a small, human-scale team, described in its own materials as 'a human-sized team,' though it does not publish an exact headcount, distinguishing it from large-scale hosting conglomerates while still serving customers ranging from individual developers to large public-sector organizations.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Alwaysdata
paragraphs: Alwaysdata is a French web hosting company, operating since 2006 and based in Paris, that provides shared, managed cloud, and dedicated hosting aimed specifically at developers and technical teams.
Since 2012 the company has been fully focused on its alwaysdata hosting platform, which it positions as a 'European cloud' offering data residency within France and the EU.

It serves a range of customers from individual developers and students on its free tier to web agencies, public administrations, and companies hosting SaaS products on its higher-end managed and dedicated plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Alwaysdata supports more than 15 programming languages and runtimes, including PHP, Python, Ruby, Node.js, Java, Go, and Perl, giving developers flexibility to deploy a wide range of application stacks.

The platform provides shell access, multiple database options, unlimited sites, email addresses, and databases with custom domains on its paid tiers, along with SSL certificates and automated daily backups included as standard.

Higher tiers scale resources significantly, from a shared free tier up to managed cloud configurations with up to 8 CPU cores and 8 GB RAM, and dedicated Max plans reaching up to 128 CPU cores and 2 TB of RAM for large-scale or high-performance workloads.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Alwaysdata offers a genuinely free, permanent tier with 1 GB of SSD storage, 256 MB RAM, and three days of backups, requiring no payment to use indefinitely.

The Plus plan starts at 5 euros per month (excluding VAT) for managed cloud hosting and scales up with storage, RAM, and CPU, while the Max plan starts at 369 euros per month (excluding VAT) for dedicated, guaranteed-performance environments, with all plans including SSL, backups, and transparent pricing with no hidden fees for standard features.

features.title: Support for 15 plus languages

features.title: Dedicated hosting (Max plan)

features.title: Shell access and multiple databases

features.title: Automated backups
features.description: Backup retention ranging from 3 days on the free tier up to 30 days on paid plans.

features.title: SSL certificates included
features.description: Free SSL certificates included across plans to secure hosted sites and applications.

features.title: EU-based data residency
features.description: Hosting infrastructure distributed across French data centers, appealing to organizations with EU data residency requirements.

pros: Genuinely free, permanent hosting tier with no forced trial expiration
Broad language and runtime support suited to developers and technical teams
Transparent pricing with no hidden fees for standard features like databases or extra languages
EU and French data residency, appealing for compliance-conscious organizations
Scales from a free hobby tier all the way to dedicated enterprise infrastructure on one platform
cons: Free tier resources (256 MB RAM, 1 GB storage) are quite limited for production use
Smaller company scale compared to major global cloud hosting providers may mean fewer data center regions
Company does not publicly disclose exact employee headcount, making team size hard to verify
Primarily French-market rooted, which may affect the depth of English-language community resources
Higher-end Max plans require significant budget (starting at 369 euros per month), pricing out very small teams

faqs.question: Is Alwaysdata free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Alwaysdata offers a permanent free plan with 1 GB SSD storage, 256 MB RAM, and basic backups, usable indefinitely without payment.

faqs.question: When was Alwaysdata founded?
faqs.answer: Alwaysdata has operated since 2006 and has been fully dedicated to its hosting platform since 2012, headquartered in Paris, France.

faqs.question: What programming languages does Alwaysdata support?
faqs.answer: It supports more than 15 languages and runtimes, including PHP, Python, Ruby, Node.js, Java, Go, and Perl.

faqs.question: How much does Alwaysdata's paid hosting cost?
faqs.answer: The Plus (managed cloud) plan starts at 5 euros per month excluding VAT, and the Max (dedicated) plan starts at 369 euros per month excluding VAT.

faqs.question: Where is Alwaysdata's data hosted?
faqs.answer: Alwaysdata hosts data in datacenters located in France, appealing to organizations that require French or EU data residency.

faqs.question: Who is Alwaysdata designed for?
faqs.answer: It targets developers, web agencies, small and mid-sized businesses, public administrations, and companies hosting SaaS infrastructure.

faqs.question: Does Alwaysdata include SSL and backups by default?
faqs.answer: Yes, SSL certificates and automated backups (ranging from 3 to 30 days depending on plan) are included as standard features.

faqs.question: Is Alwaysdata only available in French?
faqs.answer: No, the platform and website are available in both French and English.

use_cases.title: Hosting for freelance developers and agencies
use_cases.description: Manage multiple client sites, databases, and email addresses under one managed cloud plan.

use_cases.title: EU-compliant public sector hosting
use_cases.description: Host administrative or public service applications on infrastructure located within France and the EU.

use_cases.title: SaaS infrastructure hosting
use_cases.description: Run backend modules and infrastructure for a software-as-a-service product on scalable managed or dedicated plans.
